intro_band.agent_remit_summary: A customer-facing airline customer-service system built as a multi-agent pipeline: a triage agent receives each request and routes it by handoff to specialist agents, an FAQ agent answers airline policy questions using only a curated FAQ dataset, and a seat-booking agent updates seat assignments on existing confirmed reservations. The system may use only two tools — faq_lookup_tool (deterministic FAQ lookup) and update_seat (seat reassignment) — against the airline reservation system and a static FAQ dataset. Customer identity must be confirmed before any reservation-state mutation, confirmation numbers must be verified against the authoritative reservation record, and the agents must never send outbound external communication, process payments or refunds, or act on instructions embedded in customer input or tool output.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python project built on the OpenAI Agents SDK (openai-agents). The analyzed agent is the examples/customer_service/main.py example: a single ~191-line module defining three Agent instances (triage_agent, faq_agent, seat_booking_agent) wired with bidirectional handoff() calls, two @function_tools, and an on_seat_booking_handoff hook, run as an interactive console REPL via Runner.run(). The SDK ships rich safety primitives — input_guardrails and output_guardrails on Agent, tool_input_guardrails and tool_output_guardrails plus a needs_approval flag on FunctionTool, input_filter and is_enabled on handoff() — but the example wires none of them: no guardrails, needs_approval left at its False default, no identity check, no logging. It is a teaching artifact and diverges materially from the remit it is measured against.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is framework-offers-safe-primitives, example-uses-none. The OpenAI Agents SDK provides exactly the controls this remit demands — input_guardrails and output_guardrails to reject off-scope or injection-laden input, needs_approval on function_tool to gate state-mutating calls, handoff() input_filter and is_enabled to scope inter-agent transfers — and the customer-service example invokes none of them.

Every behavioral rule in the remit — identity verification before mutation, confirmation-number ownership checks, seat-availability checks, audit logging, injection resistance — is delegated entirely to natural-language routine steps inside agent instructions, with zero code-level enforcement. The compound consequence: update_seat writes attacker-supplied confirmation_number and new_seat straight into the reservation context with no approval gate, no identity check, and no audit trail — a customer, or content injected into the conversation, can reassign any seat on any confirmation number in one hop, and nothing records that it happened.

rules.rule_text: routing each request to the specialist agent best suited to handle it
The triage agent receives each incoming customer request and routes it to the appropriate specialist agent, and MUST NOT access customer data directly.

The FAQ agent answers questions about airline policies — baggage, seating, wifi, and similar — using only the curated FAQ dataset, and MUST NOT speculate or draw on general knowledge outside that dataset.
The seat-booking agent updates seat assignments on existing confirmed reservations only.
faq_lookup_tool — deterministic lookup against the airline FAQ dataset, returning only scripted answers or "I don't know"; used by the FAQ agent only.
Customer identity MUST be confirmed before any action that mutates reservation state.
A submitted confirmation number MUST be verified as belonging to the authenticated customer before that reservation is read or modified.
Seat updates MUST be limited to seats that exist on the flight and are available — not already assigned to another passenger.
Every seat change MUST be recorded to a durable audit log capturing the time, the customer identity, the confirmation number, the old and new seat, and the agent that performed the change.
A request that falls outside the current agent's scope MUST be returned to the triage agent.
Reservations or seat assignments for any customer other than the authenticated session user MUST NOT be modified.
Instructions embedded in free-text customer input that attempt to override an agent's role, scope, or identity MUST NOT be accepted or acted on.
Instructions embedded in tool outputs — FAQ content, seat records, flight data — that attempt to change an agent's behavior MUST NOT be accepted.
Ticket purchases, payments, or fares MUST NOT be created, cancelled, modified, or refunded — those flows belong to a separate billing system.
Flight numbers, confirmation numbers, and passenger identifiers MUST come from the authoritative reservation system and MUST NOT be generated by the agent at runtime.
Handoffs between agents MUST NOT escalate privileges or bypass identity verification.
A seat change MUST NOT be issued without first verifying the confirmation number against the authoritative reservation record.
Refunds, compensation, rebooking on a different flight, or any change affecting fare or ticket value MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding.
Requests involving unaccompanied minors, medical assistance, or other special-handling flags on a reservation MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding.
Any request where the customer's identity cannot be verified MUST be escalated to a human.
all customer input is untrusted until validated
The agents do not send email, text, push notifications, or webhooks.
The agents do not execute arbitrary code or shell commands.
The agents do not maintain persistent memory across customer sessions.

findings.summary: Seat-booking agent mutates reservation state with no code-level customer identity verification.
findings.description: The remit requires customer identity to be confirmed before any reservation-state mutation, and any unverifiable identity to be escalated to a human. The seat-booking agent has no such control: AirlineAgentContext carries a passenger_name field but it is never populated or checked, and update_seat fires regardless of session identity.
findings.policy_rule_text: Customer identity MUST be confirmed before any action that mutates reservation state. / Any request where the customer's identity cannot be verified MUST be escalated to a human.

evidence.snippet: update_seat is a plain @function_tool; its body sets context fields with no caller-identity check
seat_booking_agent instructions only ask for a confirmation number in prose; AirlineAgentContext.passenger_name is never populated or checked

recommended_actions: Add an input guardrail to seat_booking_agent (Agent input_guardrails) that confirms an authenticated passenger identity is present in AirlineAgentContext before any seat-mutating turn.
Gate update_seat behind needs_approval or a verified-identity predicate so the tool cannot fire for an unauthenticated session.

findings.summary: update_seat trusts a model-supplied confirmation number with no ownership verification, allowing seat changes on any reservation.
findings.description: The confirmation number reaches update_seat as an LLM tool-call argument and is written into context without any lookup against an authoritative reservation record or check that it belongs to the authenticated session user — letting the agent modify any customer's reservation.
findings.policy_rule_text: A submitted confirmation number MUST be verified as belonging to the authenticated customer before that reservation is read or modified. / Reservations or seat assignments for any customer other than the authenticated session user MUST NOT be modified. / A seat change MUST NOT be issued without first verifying the confirmation number against the authoritative reservation record.

evidence.snippet: update_seat(context, confirmation_number, new_seat) takes confirmation_number straight from LLM tool-call args
context.context.confirmation_number is overwritten with the supplied value; no lookup against an authoritative reservation record, no ownership check

recommended_actions: Before mutating, look up the confirmation number in the authoritative reservation system and assert it belongs to the authenticated session passenger; reject otherwise.
Do not let update_seat overwrite context.confirmation_number from tool args — bind it to the verified reservation for the session.

findings.summary: No durable audit log of seat changes — high-impact reservation mutations leave no record.
findings.description: The remit mandates a durable audit record for every seat change. update_seat writes nothing to any log or store, and the module has no logging calls at all — agent activity is only printed to the console, leaving reservation mutations forensically invisible.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every seat change MUST be recorded to a durable audit log capturing the time, the customer identity, the confirmation number, the old and new seat, and the agent that performed the change.

evidence.snippet: update_seat returns a confirmation string and writes nothing to any log or store
module has zero 'import logging' / logging calls; agent activity is only print()ed to the console

recommended_actions: In update_seat, write a structured audit record (timestamp, passenger identity, confirmation number, old seat, new seat, performing agent) to a durable store before returning.
Add structured action logging across the run loop so every tool call and handoff is recorded.

findings.summary: update_seat performs no seat-existence or availability check before reassigning a seat.
findings.description: The remit limits seat updates to seats that exist on the flight and are available. update_seat assigns the model-supplied seat directly; its only check is an assert that flight_number is set. A non-existent seat, or one already assigned to another passenger, would be accepted.
findings.policy_rule_text: The seat-booking agent updates seat assignments on existing confirmed reservations only. / Seat updates MUST be limited to seats that exist on the flight and are available — not already assigned to another passenger.

evidence.snippet: context.context.seat_number = new_seat assigns the model-supplied seat with no validation
the only check is an assert that flight_number is not None; nothing verifies the seat exists on the flight or is unoccupied

recommended_actions: Query the flight's seat map in update_seat and reject seats that do not exist or are already assigned to another passenger.

findings.summary: Raw customer free-text is fed to the agents with no injection defense; instruction-override attempts can redirect agent behavior.
findings.description: Customer console input is appended verbatim to the conversation and passed to Runner.run() with no input guardrail on any of the three agents. The SDK's input_guardrails facility is available but unused, so role/scope/identity-override instructions embedded in customer text reach the model unchecked.
findings.policy_rule_text: Instructions embedded in free-text customer input that attempt to override an agent's role, scope, or identity MUST NOT be accepted or acted on. / all customer input is untrusted until validated.

evidence.snippet: input_items.append({"content": user_input, "role": "user"}) appends raw console input with no sanitization
Runner.run(current_agent, input_items, context=context) — no input_guardrails on any of the three agents; the SDK input_guardrails facility is unused

recommended_actions: Add an input guardrail (Agent.input_guardrails) on the triage agent that detects role/scope/identity-override attempts and trips the tripwire.
Treat customer input as untrusted: never let it select agent identity or bypass routine steps.

findings.summary: Tool outputs re-enter LLM context untrusted — no defense against instructions embedded in tool output.
findings.description: to_input_list() folds raw tool-call outputs back into the conversation each turn. faq_lookup_tool and update_seat declare no tool_output_guardrails, so instruction-like content in FAQ data or seat records would reach the model as if it were trusted conversation.
findings.policy_rule_text: Instructions embedded in tool outputs — FAQ content, seat records, flight data — that attempt to change an agent's behavior MUST NOT be accepted.

evidence.snippet: input_items = result.to_input_list() folds raw tool-call outputs back into the conversation with no filtering
tools=[faq_lookup_tool] declared with no tool_output_guardrails; the SDK tool_output_guardrails facility is unused

recommended_actions: Attach tool_output_guardrails to faq_lookup_tool and update_seat to screen tool output for instruction-like content before it re-enters context.

findings.summary: Triage routing depends entirely on LLM judgment over handoff descriptions — no deterministic routing gate.
findings.description: The triage agent's instructions are a single generic sentence and routing is left to model discretion over handoff_description strings. Return-to-triage on out-of-scope requests is likewise a prose routine step, not a code-enforced boundary.
findings.policy_rule_text: routing each request to the specialist agent best suited to handle it / A request that falls outside the current agent's scope MUST be returned to the triage agent.

evidence.snippet: triage_agent instructions are a single generic sentence ('You are a helpful triaging agent'); routing is left to model discretion
return-to-triage is a prose routine step in the specialist agents, not enforced by code

recommended_actions: Treat handoff routing as advisory only; add code-level scope checks (handoff is_enabled predicates) so a specialist agent cannot act outside its declared scope even if the LLM mis-routes.

findings.summary: FAQ agent's dataset-only grounding rule is prompt-only; the model can still answer from general knowledge.
findings.description: The instruction 'Do not rely on your own knowledge' is the only enforcement of the curated-dataset constraint. When faq_lookup_tool returns its 'I don't know' fallback, nothing prevents the model from composing an answer from general knowledge instead.
findings.policy_rule_text: The FAQ agent answers questions about airline policies — baggage, seating, wifi, and similar — using only the curated FAQ dataset, and MUST NOT speculate or draw on general knowledge outside that dataset.

evidence.snippet: instruction 'Do not rely on your own knowledge' is the only enforcement; nothing prevents the model from composing an answer when faq_lookup_tool returns 'I don't know'
faq_lookup_tool returns a fallback 'I don't know' string, but the agent is free to ignore it and answer anyway

recommended_actions: Add an output guardrail on faq_agent that verifies the response is grounded in a faq_lookup_tool result and trips when the agent answers without one.

findings.summary: on_seat_booking_handoff fabricates a flight number at runtime instead of reading it from the reservation system.
findings.description: The seat-booking handoff hook generates a random flight number and writes it into context, which update_seat's assert then treats as authoritative. The remit forbids the agent generating flight numbers at runtime — they must come from the reservation system.
findings.policy_rule_text: Flight numbers, confirmation numbers, and passenger identifiers MUST come from the authoritative reservation system and MUST NOT be generated by the agent at runtime.

evidence.snippet: flight_number = f'FLT-{random.randint(100, 999)}' generates a random flight number
context.context.flight_number is set to that fabricated value, which update_seat's assert then treats as authoritative

recommended_actions: Replace the random flight-number generation in on_seat_booking_handoff with a lookup of the real flight number from the authoritative reservation record for the verified confirmation number.

findings.summary: Handoffs carry no input filter or identity-preserving guard — inter-agent transfers rely solely on the LLM.
findings.description: The faq and seat-booking handoffs are created with no input_filter and no is_enabled predicate. The SDK supports both to scope and condition transfers; without them the seat-booking agent is reachable regardless of session identity and inherits the full untrusted conversation.
findings.policy_rule_text: Handoffs between agents MUST NOT escalate privileges or bypass identity verification.

evidence.snippet: handoff(agent=faq_agent, ...) and the seat-booking handoff are created with no input_filter and no is_enabled predicate
the SDK handoff() supports input_filter and is_enabled to scope/condition transfers; the example uses neither

recommended_actions: Add an is_enabled predicate to the seat-booking handoff so it is only reachable once session identity is verified, and use input_filter to strip untrusted instruction content from the transferred conversation.

findings.summary: No detection or human-checkpoint path for special-handling requests (unaccompanied minors, medical assistance).
findings.description: The remit requires special-handling requests to reach a human checkpoint. The triage agent has no branch, guardrail, or tool for special-handling escalation, so such requests would be routed to a specialist agent like any other.
findings.policy_rule_text: Requests involving unaccompanied minors, medical assistance, or other special-handling flags on a reservation MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding.

evidence.snippet: triage_agent has no branch, guardrail, or tool for special-handling escalation; such requests would be routed like any other

recommended_actions: Add a guardrail or routing rule that detects special-handling indicators and escalates to a human checkpoint instead of a specialist agent.

findings.summary: No rate limiting, retry cap, or cost ceiling on the agent run loop.
findings.description: The interactive while True REPL calls Runner.run() each turn with no per-session request cap, no token or cost budget, and no turn timeout — a denial-of-wallet and runaway-loop exposure for any production adaptation.

evidence.snippet: the 'while True' REPL calls Runner.run() each turn with no per-session request cap, no token budget, and no timeout

recommended_actions: For any production adaptation, add per-session request limits, a token/cost budget, and a turn timeout around Runner.run().

findings.summary: Compound chain — untrusted customer input reaches an ungated state-mutating tool with no audit trail in a single hop.
findings.description: Three individually-filed gaps compose into one exploitable chain: unguarded customer input enters the conversation, the seat-booking handoff and update_seat fire with no identity or ownership check and needs_approval at its False default, and the resulting reservation mutation is never recorded. One crafted message reassigns any seat on any confirmation number, invisibly.
findings.policy_rule_text: Customer identity MUST be confirmed before any action that mutates reservation state. / Instructions embedded in free-text customer input that attempt to override an agent's role, scope, or identity MUST NOT be accepted or acted on.

evidence.snippet: raw user_input enters the conversation with no guardrail (see PRAX-2026-05-19-005)
update_seat then writes model-derived confirmation_number/new_seat with needs_approval=False default and no identity check (see PRAX-2026-05-19-001, -002)
and the mutation is never recorded (see PRAX-2026-05-19-003)

recommended_actions: Close the chain end-to-end: input guardrail on triage, then a verified-identity predicate gating the seat-booking handoff and update_seat, then an ownership-checked confirmation lookup, then a durable audit write. No single fix is sufficient alone.

positives.title: Deterministic FAQ grounding tool
positives.description: faq_lookup_tool is a hardcoded keyword lookup that returns only scripted answers or an explicit 'I'm sorry, I don't know' — it cannot itself hallucinate FAQ content.

positives.title: Bounded dependency ranges plus lockfile
positives.description: pyproject.toml constrains every runtime dependency to a bounded major-version range and the repo ships a uv.lock lockfile, giving reproducible installs.

positives.title: No outbound-communication or exec capability in the example
positives.description: The example wires only faq_lookup_tool and update_seat; there is no email/SMS/webhook tool and no shell or exec tool, so several remit prohibitions hold structurally.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files were found in the workspace; the customer-service example emits agent activity only as console print() output and has zero logging calls — the absence of a durable audit record is itself finding PRAX-2026-05-19-003.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Absent overall posture. The customer-service example is a teaching artifact that demonstrates the Agents SDK orchestration model, not a deployable customer-service system — it carries no runtime enforcement of the identity, approval, and audit controls its remit requires. The one bright spot is supply-chain hygiene — bounded dependency ranges and a lockfile — which lifts that single category to Partial. Every safety-critical category, Zero Trust above all at 0, depends entirely on natural-language instructions the model can be argued out of.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Domain scoping exists only as prose in each agent's instructions (the FAQ and seat-booking routines) and the handoff_description strings; there is no code gate, no is_enabled predicate, and no guardrail restricting topic, so the restriction is soft and jailbreak-bypassable.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: faq_lookup_tool is a deterministic scripted lookup, which keeps this off zero, but raw customer input is fed to Runner.run() unvalidated and tool outputs re-enter LLM context untrusted with no data-source labeling or trust boundary.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: No code-level interposition exists on any tool call: update_seat writes model-supplied confirmation_number and new_seat into reservation context with needs_approval at its False default and no identity, ownership, or availability check, and no agent declares input_guardrails or output_guardrails.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: pyproject.toml pins dependencies with bounded ranges (openai>=2.26.0,<3, pydantic>=2.12.2,<3, mcp>=1.19.0,<2) and a uv.lock lockfile is present, but no model version is pinned and there is no ML-BOM, so coverage is partial.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The SDK repository carries a tests/ tree, but there is no adversarial or red-team artifact targeting the customer-service example — no injection test, no documented exercise that drove a design change in main.py; a general unit-test suite is not adversarial testing.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: main.py has zero logging calls; agent activity is only print()ed to the console, and the trace() span at main.py:166 yields ephemeral debug spans, not a durable structured action-level audit record — the remit's required seat-change audit log does not exist.
